title: Authentication & Access Control
description: Master authentication mechanisms, password security, session management, authorisation models, and access control — covering CWE-287 (Improper Authentication), CWE-862 (Missing Authorisation), CWE-798 (Hard-coded Credentials), CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function), and CWE-269 (Improper Privilege Management).
learning_outcomes: Explain the difference between authentication and authorisation
Implement secure password hashing with salts using Java
Identify and fix hard-coded credential vulnerabilities (CWE-798)
Build a role-based access control (RBAC) system
Detect and prevent brute-force login attacks
Apply the principle of least privilege in code
Build secure login, logout, and password reset flows
Implement account lockout and rate limiting

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Thinks of authentication like a bouncer at a club — checking your ID before letting you in!

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Authentication can feel like a lot of moving parts. Think of it like entering a building: first you PROVE who you are (authentication), then someone checks what rooms you can enter (authorisation). Want me to walk through it?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? That is completely fine — security is layered! Let me simplify: Authentication = 'Who are you?' Authorisation = 'What are you allowed to do?' Two separate questions, two separate checks in your code.
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No worries at all! Let us start fresh. Imagine a hotel: you show your ID at reception (authentication), they give you a keycard (session), and the keycard only opens YOUR room (authorisation). Every web app works the same way. Ready to try again?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good attempt! Authentication bugs are very common in real software. Let us check your logic together — usually it is a missing check or wrong comparison.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: I can see you are working hard. Let me show you a minimal working example, then we will build up step by step.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You just secured an authentication flow. Real-world breaches happen because developers skip these exact checks — you are ahead of the curve!

encouragement_phrases: Authentication is the front door of every application. You are learning to build strong doors!
Every password you hash properly protects a real person's account!
Access control bugs are the #1 vulnerability in OWASP Top 10. You are tackling the biggest threat!
The difference between a secure app and a breached one is often just ONE missing check. You are learning to find them!
Hard-coded passwords have caused massive breaches. You now know how to avoid them!
Role-based access control is used in every enterprise application. These are career-level skills!
Session management is tricky but you are getting it. Every login flow uses what you are learning!
Brute-force protection saves millions of accounts from automated attacks. Great work!
Least privilege means giving minimum access needed. You are thinking like a security architect!
Multi-factor authentication stops 99% of account takeovers. You understand why!

explanation_styles.analogy: Authentication is like a bouncer checking your ID at a nightclub. Authorisation is like the VIP wristband that lets you into the exclusive area. You need BOTH: the bouncer must verify you are who you say you are, AND the wristband controls where you can go.
explanation_styles.visual: Draw the flow: [User enters credentials] -> [Server checks hash] -> [Session created] -> [Each request: check session + check permissions] -> [Allow or Deny]. Every step is a security gate.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: User sends username + password. Step 2: Server looks up stored hash for that username. Step 3: Server hashes the submitted password with the same salt. Step 4: Compare hashes — if match, create session. Step 5: On every subsequent request, check session is valid AND user has permission for that action.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Line 1: Get username and password from request. Line 2: Query database for user record. Line 3: Get stored salt and hash. Line 4: Hash submitted password with stored salt. Line 5: Compare computed hash with stored hash. Line 6: If match, create session token. Line 7: Store session with user ID and role.
explanation_styles.comparison: WITHOUT proper auth: anyone can access any page, any data, any function. WITH proper auth: each user is verified, each action is authorised, each session is tracked. The difference between chaos and order.

lessons.title: Authentication & Access Control: From Passwords to Permissions

theory.introduction: Every time you log into your email, bank, or social media, you go through authentication — the process of proving you are who you claim to be. But logging in is only half the story. After you are authenticated, the system must also decide what you are ALLOWED to do. Can you read other people's messages? Can you delete accounts? Can you access the admin panel? This is authorisation — controlling what authenticated users can access. Getting authentication wrong means attackers can pretend to be anyone. Getting authorisation wrong means a regular user can act like an administrator. Both are devastating. In the OWASP Top 10, Broken Access Control is the NUMBER ONE vulnerability, and Identification and Authentication Failures is in the top 10 as well. This course teaches you to build both correctly from scratch: secure password storage, session management, role-based access control, and all the common pitfalls that lead to real-world breaches. We cover CWE-287 (Improper Authentication), CWE-862 (Missing Authorisation), CWE-798 (Hard-coded Credentials), CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function), and CWE-269 (Improper Privilege Management) — the most critical weaknesses in modern applications.
theory.why_important: Broken authentication and access control are behind the majority of data breaches. The 2017 Equifax breach exposed 147 million records partly due to authentication failures. Hard-coded credentials in IoT devices have enabled massive botnets. Missing authorisation checks let attackers access admin functions by simply changing a URL parameter. These are not theoretical — they happen every day. Understanding these vulnerabilities and their fixes is essential for anyone building or testing software.

key_concepts.explanation: Authentication answers 'WHO are you?' — verifying identity through credentials like passwords, biometrics, or tokens. Authorisation answers 'WHAT can you do?' — checking permissions after identity is confirmed. Think of a hotel: showing your passport at reception is authentication; your room keycard only opening YOUR room is authorisation. Many developers confuse these or implement one without the other. Both must be present and correct.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Authentication = passport check. Authorisation = access badge. You need both. A valid passport does not mean you can enter the server room!

key_concepts.explanation: Never store passwords in plain text or even encrypted — store them HASHED. A hash is a one-way function: you can convert a password to a hash, but you cannot convert the hash back to the password. A salt is a random value added to the password before hashing, making each hash unique even for identical passwords. Without salt, attackers can use precomputed rainbow tables to crack thousands of passwords instantly. With salt, each password must be cracked individually.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of salt as a unique ingredient added to each recipe. Even if two chefs make the same dish, adding different salts means the results taste completely different — attackers cannot use a master recipe book.

key_concepts.explanation: Hard-coded credentials are passwords, API keys, or cryptographic keys embedded directly in source code. This is extremely dangerous because: (1) anyone who reads the source code knows the password, (2) the password is the same for every installation, (3) it cannot be changed without modifying code, (4) it often ends up in version control (Git) where it persists forever. Real-world examples include IoT devices with default admin/admin credentials, and applications with backdoor passwords baked into the code.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Hard-coding a password is like writing the combination to your safe on the outside of the safe. Anyone who looks can get in, and you cannot change it without replacing the whole safe.

key_concepts.explanation: Missing authorisation means a function performs a sensitive action WITHOUT checking if the current user has permission. For example, a 'delete user' endpoint that checks if the user is logged in but NOT if they are an admin. Or an API that returns any user's data by ID without checking if the requester should see that data. This is different from authentication — the user IS logged in, but the system fails to check their PERMISSIONS.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Authentication without authorisation is like letting everyone through the hotel entrance but not checking room keycards. You know WHO they are but not WHERE they should go.

key_concepts.explanation: Improper authentication means the authentication mechanism can be bypassed or does not properly verify identity. Examples: accepting an empty password, checking username but not password, using client-side validation only, comparing passwords case-insensitively, or allowing authentication bypass through parameter manipulation. The core principle: authentication must be SERVER-SIDE, must check ALL required credentials, and must use secure comparison.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Authentication must be airtight. One bypass and the entire security model collapses. Check everything on the server, never trust the client.

key_concepts.explanation: After authentication, the server creates a session — a temporary identifier that links subsequent requests to the authenticated user. Sessions must be: random (unpredictable), unique (no collisions), time-limited (expire), invalidated on logout, and transmitted securely (HTTPS, HttpOnly cookies). Poor session management leads to session hijacking (stealing the session ID), session fixation (forcing a known session ID), and session replay (reusing old sessions).
key_concepts.ai_hint: A session is like a wristband at a festival. It proves you already showed your ticket (authenticated). But it must expire, be hard to copy, and be collected when you leave.

key_concepts.explanation: RBAC assigns permissions based on roles (ADMIN, USER, MANAGER) rather than individual users. Each role has a set of allowed actions. When a user tries to do something, the system checks: (1) Is the user authenticated? (2) What role does the user have? (3) Does that role have permission for this action? RBAC simplifies permission management — instead of giving permissions to 1000 users individually, assign them to 3 roles and put users in roles.
key_concepts.ai_hint: RBAC is like a hotel key system: guests open their room, staff open all rooms on their floor, and the manager opens everything. You define the key levels, not individual door access.

key_concepts.explanation: Brute-force attacks try thousands or millions of password combinations. Without protection, an attacker can guess common passwords quickly (dictionary attack) or try every combination (brute force). Defences include: account lockout after N failed attempts, progressive delays (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s...), CAPTCHA after failed attempts, rate limiting by IP address, and monitoring for unusual login patterns. Balance security with usability — permanent lockout can be used for denial-of-service.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Brute force is like trying every key on a keyring. Account lockout is like changing the lock after too many wrong keys. Rate limiting is like making the attacker wait longer between tries.

key_concepts.explanation: Every user, program, and process should have only the minimum permissions needed to perform their function — nothing more. If a user only needs to read reports, do not give them write access. If a database connection only needs SELECT, do not grant DELETE. This limits the damage when an account is compromised. A compromised admin account is catastrophic; a compromised read-only account is a data leak but not data destruction.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Least privilege is like giving hotel staff master keys only to the floors they work on, not the entire hotel. If their key is stolen, the damage is limited.

key_concepts.explanation: IDOR happens when an application uses user-supplied input to access objects directly without checking authorisation. Example: /api/invoice?id=1001 shows invoice 1001. An attacker changes it to id=1002 and sees someone else's invoice. The fix: always check that the authenticated user is AUTHORISED to access the requested object. Never trust client-supplied IDs without server-side permission validation.
key_concepts.ai_hint: IDOR is like an apartment building where changing the number on a mailbox lets you read anyone's mail. The fix: check if the person requesting the mail actually lives in that apartment.

key_concepts.explanation: MFA requires two or more factors to authenticate: something you KNOW (password), something you HAVE (phone, hardware key), something you ARE (fingerprint, face). Even if an attacker steals your password, they cannot log in without the second factor. MFA stops 99.9%% of automated attacks. Common implementations: SMS codes, authenticator apps (TOTP), push notifications, hardware security keys (FIDO2/WebAuthn).
key_concepts.ai_hint: MFA is like needing both your key AND your fingerprint to open a door. Losing one is not enough for an attacker to get in.

key_concepts.explanation: Password reset is a critical flow that attackers target. A secure reset: (1) generates a cryptographically random token, (2) stores the token hashed (not plain text), (3) sends a link with the token to the verified email, (4) the token expires in 15-60 minutes, (5) the token is single-use, (6) requires setting a new strong password. Insecure resets: security questions (guessable), sending the password in email (visible), using predictable tokens, or not expiring tokens.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Password reset is like requesting a new house key from a locksmith. The locksmith sends a time-limited code to your verified address. You must use it quickly, and it only works once.

key_concepts.explanation: Every authentication event should be logged: successful logins, failed logins, logouts, password changes, role changes, and access denials. Logs must include: timestamp, user ID, IP address, action, and result. This enables: detecting brute-force attacks (many failed logins), identifying compromised accounts (login from unusual location), forensics after a breach, and compliance with security standards. Never log passwords or session tokens!
key_concepts.ai_hint: Audit logs are like security cameras for your code. They do not prevent break-ins, but they help you detect them and figure out what happened.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Authentication vs Authorisation Visualised
complete_examples.description: Clear demonstration of the two-step security check
complete_examples.output:
=== AUTHENTICATION vs AUTHORISATION ===

AUTHENTICATION (Who are you?)
  Input: username + password
  Check: credentials match stored record?
  Result: VERIFIED or REJECTED
  Analogy: Showing your passport at the border

AUTHORISATION (What can you do?)
  Input: user's role + requested action
  Check: does this role have permission?
  Result: ALLOWED or DENIED
  Analogy: Your security clearance level

User: alice
  Authentication: PASS
  Authorisation for DELETE_USER: ALLOWED

User: bob
  Authentication: PASS
  Authorisation for DELETE_USER: DENIED

User: eve
  Authentication: FAIL
  Authorisation: SKIPPED (not authenticated)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Three scenarios: Alice is admin (passes both), Bob is regular user (passes authn but fails authz for admin action), Eve has wrong password (fails at authn, never reaches authz). Both checks are needed.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if you check authorisation but skip authentication? Could an unauthenticated user with role 'ADMIN' in a forged cookie bypass security?

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Password Hashing with Salt
complete_examples.description: Complete implementation of secure password storage
complete_examples.output:
=== REGISTRATION ===
Password: MySecurePass123!
Salt (Base64): (random 24-char string)
Hash (Base64): (44-char hash string)
Store in DB: salt=..., hash=...

=== LOGIN VERIFICATION ===
Input: MySecurePass123!
Computed hash: (same 44-char hash)
Match: true

Wrong input: WrongPassword
Computed hash: (different hash)
Match: false

=== WHY SALT MATTERS ===
Same password, salt 1: (hash A)
Same password, salt 2: (hash B)
Different hashes: true
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Salt ensures that even identical passwords produce different hashes. Registration: generate salt, hash password+salt, store both. Login: retrieve stored salt, hash input+salt, compare with stored hash. The password is never stored or compared directly.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Why is SHA-256 alone not ideal for passwords? Research bcrypt or PBKDF2 and explain why they are better for password hashing.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Hard-coded Credentials Detector
complete_examples.description: Finding and fixing CWE-798 in Java source code
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The detector uses regex patterns to find common hard-coded credential patterns: direct string assignments to password/secret variables, and .equals() comparisons with literal strings. Safe code uses environment variables or config files instead.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What other patterns might indicate hard-coded credentials? Think about Base64-encoded strings, hex values, and connection strings.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Complete RBAC System
complete_examples.description: Full role-based access control with permissions matrix
complete_examples.output:
=== RBAC ACCESS MATRIX ===
          DELETE_USER         VIEW_REPORTS        EDIT_OWN_PROFILE    VIEW_PUBLIC_PAGE    
alice(A)  ALLOWED             ALLOWED             DENIED              DENIED              
bob(M)    DENIED              ALLOWED             DENIED              DENIED              
charlie(U)DENIED              ALLOWED             ALLOWED             DENIED              
guest1(G) DENIED              DENIED              DENIED              ALLOWED             
unknown(N)DENIED              DENIED              DENIED              DENIED
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The RBAC matrix shows exactly which roles can perform which actions. Admin has the most permissions, Guest the least. Unknown users have NO permissions. Each user is mapped to a role, and each role defines allowed actions.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a MODERATOR role that can VIEW_USERS and DELETE_USER but not MANAGE_SETTINGS. Where does it fit in the hierarchy?

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Brute-Force Login Protection
complete_examples.output:
=== BRUTE FORCE PROTECTION ===
FAILED: 2 attempts remaining
FAILED: 1 attempts remaining
LOCKED: 3 failed attempts. Account locked for 30 seconds.
LOCKED: Try again in 29 seconds

After lockout expires:
SUCCESS: Welcome alice
complete_examples.ai_explanation: After 3 failed attempts, the account locks for 30 seconds. Even the correct password is rejected during lockout. After the lockout expires, normal login resumes. This stops automated brute-force attacks while allowing legitimate users to try again.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if an attacker locks out everyone's account on purpose (denial of service)? How would you balance security with availability?

complete_examples.title: Example 6: IDOR Vulnerability and Fix
complete_examples.description: How changing an ID in a URL exposes other users' data
complete_examples.output:
=== IDOR VULNERABILITY ===
Alice requests her invoice:
  Invoice INV-001: Laptop - $500
Alice changes ID to see Bob's invoice:
  Invoice INV-002: Server - $1200
  (BUG: Alice can see Bob's data!)

=== FIXED VERSION ===
Alice requests her invoice:
  Invoice INV-001: Laptop - $500
Alice tries Bob's invoice:
  ACCESS DENIED: This invoice belongs to bob, not alice
complete_examples.ai_explanation: IDOR is one of the most common vulnerabilities. The fix is simple: always verify that the authenticated user OWNS or has PERMISSION to access the requested resource. Never trust client-supplied IDs without checking.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if invoices can be shared? Design a permission model where the owner can grant read access to specific other users.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Session Management Lifecycle
complete_examples.description: Complete session creation, validation, and destruction
complete_examples.output:
=== SESSION LIFECYCLE ===
Created: kX7vR2mP9qYt...
Valid: true User: alice
Invalid ID: false
After logout: false
Active sessions: 0
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Sessions have a complete lifecycle: create on login (with secure random ID), validate on each request (check timeout), destroy on logout. Two timeout types: absolute (max session age) and idle (inactivity). Both prevent session reuse after the user is done.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What is session fixation? How would you prevent an attacker from forcing a known session ID?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Secure Login Flow
complete_examples.description: Complete login with hashing, brute-force protection, and session creation
complete_examples.output:
=== SECURE LOGIN FLOW ===
SUCCESS: Session=kX7vR2mP9qYt...
FAILED: Invalid credentials
FAILED: Invalid credentials
SUCCESS: Session=pQ8rT3nU6yWa...
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This combines all secure login practices: password hashing with salt, generic error messages (same for bad username and bad password to prevent user enumeration), brute-force lockout, and secure session creation. Notice how 'nonexistent' and 'wrong password' give the SAME error.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Why is it important to give the same error for 'user not found' and 'wrong password'? What could an attacker learn from different messages?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Missing Authorisation Demo
complete_examples.description: Showing CWE-862 in action and how to fix it
complete_examples.output:
=== CWE-862: MISSING AUTHORISATION ===

VULNERABLE (any logged-in user can delete):
  DELETED: dave (by bob)

FIXED (only admin can delete):
  DENIED: bob is not an admin (role: USER)
  DELETED: dave (by admin alice)
  DENIED: Cannot delete yourself
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The vulnerable version checks IF the user is logged in but not WHAT they can do. Bob (a regular USER) can delete anyone. The fixed version checks the role AND prevents self-deletion. This is the essence of CWE-862.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What other sensitive actions commonly miss authorisation checks? Think about: viewing other users' data, changing settings, accessing admin panels.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: MFA Code Generation and Verification
complete_examples.description: Implementing two-factor authentication with time-limited codes
complete_examples.output:
=== MULTI-FACTOR AUTHENTICATION ===

Step 1: Password verified for alice
Step 2: MFA code sent to alice's phone: 847293

Step 3: Alice enters wrong code:
  WRONG: Incorrect code

Step 4: Alice enters correct code:
  VERIFIED: MFA complete

Step 5: Replay attack (reuse code):
  INVALID: No code generated
complete_examples.ai_explanation: MFA adds a second layer: after password verification, a one-time code is sent to the user's phone. The code expires after 5 minutes, is single-use (removed after verification), and must match exactly. Even if an attacker knows the password, they cannot complete login without the phone.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: SMS-based MFA has weaknesses (SIM swapping). Research TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) used by Google Authenticator. How is it more secure?

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Password Strength Validator
complete_examples.output:
Password: "password123"
  FAIL: Missing uppercase letter
  FAIL: Missing special character
  FAIL: Contains common password: password

Password: "Short1!"
  FAIL: Too short (min 8 characters)

Password: "NoSpecial123"
  FAIL: Missing special character

Password: "Str0ng!Pass#2024"
  STRONG

Password: "admin"
  FAIL: Too short (min 8 characters)
  FAIL: Missing uppercase letter
  FAIL: Missing digit
  FAIL: Missing special character
  FAIL: Contains common password: admin
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Password validation checks length, character types, and common passwords. Multiple checks run independently so the user sees ALL issues at once. Note: password strength rules alone are not enough — always combine with hashing, salting, and brute-force protection.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Research why some security experts now recommend LONG passphrases (like 'correct horse battery staple') over complex short passwords. Which is easier to remember AND harder to crack?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Audit Log Implementation
complete_examples.description: Logging all authentication events for security monitoring
complete_examples.output:
=== AUTHENTICATION AUDIT LOG ===
[2026-02-23T14:30:00] LOGIN user=alice ip=10.0.0.1 result=SUCCESS detail=normal
[2026-02-23T14:30:01] LOGIN user=bob ip=45.33.22.1 result=FAILED detail=wrong_password
[2026-02-23T14:30:02] LOGIN user=bob ip=45.33.22.1 result=FAILED detail=wrong_password
[2026-02-23T14:30:03] LOGIN user=bob ip=45.33.22.1 result=FAILED detail=wrong_password
[2026-02-23T14:30:04] LOGIN user=admin ip=45.33.22.1 result=FAILED detail=wrong_password
[2026-02-23T14:30:05] LOGIN user=root ip=45.33.22.1 result=FAILED detail=wrong_password
[2026-02-23T14:30:06] ROLE_CHANGE user=alice ip=10.0.0.1 result=SUCCESS detail=bob:USER->ADMIN
[2026-02-23T14:30:07] LOGOUT user=alice ip=10.0.0.1 result=SUCCESS detail=normal

=== ANOMALY DETECTION ===
ALERT: User 'bob' has 3 failed logins (brute force?)
ALERT: IP 45.33.22.1 has 5 failed logins (credential stuffing?)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The audit log records every authentication event. Anomaly detection spots: brute-force (many failures for one user) and credential stuffing (many failures from one IP across different users). Role changes are logged for accountability.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What should you NEVER log? Think about passwords, tokens, and personal data. What compliance regulations affect logging (GDPR, PCI-DSS)?

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Privilege Escalation Prevention
complete_examples.output:
=== PRIVILEGE ESCALATION PREVENTION ===

VULNERABLE:
  Changed charlie to ADMIN

SECURE:
  DENIED: Only admins can change roles
  DENIED: Only admins can change roles
  DENIED: Cannot assign role equal to or above your own
  SUCCESS: charlie changed from USER to MANAGER (by alice)
  DENIED: Cannot change own role
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Privilege escalation prevention requires: only admins change roles, cannot promote above your own level, cannot change your own role. The vulnerable version lets ANYONE change ANY role — charlie promotes himself to admin!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Design a system where role changes require approval from TWO admins (dual control). How would you implement this?

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Complete Security Assessment Checklist
complete_examples.description: Evaluating authentication and access control implementation
complete_examples.output:
=== AUTHENTICATION & ACCESS CONTROL ASSESSMENT ===

AUTHENTICATION:
  [PASS] Passwords hashed with salt
  [PASS] Uses bcrypt/PBKDF2/Argon2
  [PASS] No hard-coded credentials
  [PASS] Generic error messages
  [PASS] Brute-force protection
  [FAIL] MFA available
  [PASS] Secure password reset
  [PASS] Password strength requirements

SESSION MANAGEMENT:
  [PASS] Session IDs random
  [PASS] Absolute timeout
  [FAIL] Idle timeout
  [PASS] Destroyed on logout
  [PASS] New ID after login
  [PASS] Cookie attributes

ACCESS CONTROL:
  [PASS] RBAC implemented
  [PASS] Authorisation every request
  [FAIL] IDOR prevention
  [PASS] Least privilege
  [PASS] Admin protected
  [PASS] Escalation prevented

AUDIT:
  [PASS] Logins logged
  [PASS] Denials logged
  [FAIL] Role changes logged
  [PASS] Passwords not logged

SCORE: 20/24 - EXCELLENT
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This checklist covers all four pillars: authentication, session management, access control, and audit logging. A real security assessment would check each item with penetration testing. The 4 failures (MFA, idle timeout, IDOR, role change logging) represent common gaps.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Which of the 4 failures would you prioritise fixing first? Consider: IDOR directly exposes user data, missing MFA enables account takeover, missing idle timeout allows session reuse.

visual_diagram.title: Authentication & Access Control: Complete Security Flow
visual_diagram.content: [AUTHENTICATION FLOW] STEP 1 User submits username + password | v STEP 2 Server looks up user record in database User not found? -> Return generic error (no user enumeration!) | v STEP 3 Hash submitted password with stored salt password + salt -> SHA-256 -> computed hash | v STEP 4 Compare computed hash with stored hash Match? -> Create session, return session ID No match? -> Increment fail count, check lockout [AUTHORISATION CHECK (Every Request)] CHECK 1 Is the session valid? (exists + not expired) Invalid -> 401 Unauthorised | v CHECK 2 What role does this user have? Look up role from session: ADMIN / MANAGER / USER | v CHECK 3 Does this role have permission for this action? Yes -> Process request No -> 403 Forbidden | v CHECK 4 Resource ownership (IDOR prevention) User owns resource? -> Return data Different owner? -> 403 Forbidden [CWE MAPPING] CWE-287 Improper Authentication - login can be bypassed CWE-798 Hard-coded Credentials - passwords in source code CWE-862 Missing Authorisation - no permission check CWE-306 Missing Auth for Critical Function - admin action unprotected CWE-269 Improper Privilege Management - users can escalate roles [REMEMBER] 1. Authentication = WHO are you? Authorisation = WHAT can you do? 2. NEVER store plain text passwords. Hash + Salt always. 3. NEVER hard-code credentials. Use environment variables. 4. Check permissions on EVERY request, not just the UI. 5. Same error for bad username and bad password (no enumeration). 6. Sessions: random IDs, timeouts, destroy on logout. 7. IDOR: always verify resource ownership. 8. Audit log everything. Never log passwords.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Auth vs Authz Quiz
practice_exercises.description: Classify each scenario as authentication or authorisation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. AUTH_N
2. AUTH_Z
3. AUTH_N
4. AUTH_Z
5. AUTH_N
6. AUTH_Z
7. AUTH_N
8. AUTH_Z
9. AUTH_N
10. AUTH_Z

hints.message: Authentication verifies IDENTITY. Authorisation checks PERMISSIONS.
Passwords, tokens, fingerprints, API keys = identity verification = AUTH_N. Roles, ownership, actions = permission checks = AUTH_Z.
1.AUTH_N 2.AUTH_Z 3.AUTH_N 4.AUTH_Z 5.AUTH_N 6.AUTH_Z 7.AUTH_N 8.AUTH_Z 9.AUTH_N 10.AUTH_Z

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Spot the CWE
practice_exercises.description: Identify which CWE each vulnerability represents.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. CWE-798 (Hard-coded Credentials)
2. CWE-862 (Missing Authorisation)
3. CWE-287 (Improper Authentication)
4. CWE-306 (Missing Auth for Critical Function)
5. CWE-269 (Improper Privilege Management)

hints.message: 798=hard-coded, 862=missing authz, 287=improper authn, 306=no auth on critical func, 269=privilege escalation.
Hard-coded password->798. No role check->862. Empty password accepted->287. Admin with no login->306. Self-promote->269.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: HTTP Status Codes
practice_exercises.description: Match security responses to correct HTTP codes.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. 401 (not authenticated)
2. 403 (authenticated but not authorised)
3. 200 (success)
4. 403 (not authorised for this resource)
5. 404 (not found)

hints.message: 401 = not logged in at all. 403 = logged in but no permission.
401 is about identity. 403 is about permissions. Both are denials but for different reasons.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Why Not Plain Text?
practice_exercises.description: Explain what happens when plain text passwords are stolen.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
If attacker steals database:
  1. Every password visible immediately: yes
  2. Users with same password identifiable: yes
  3. Passwords usable on other sites: yes (password reuse)
  4. Fix requires all users to change password: yes

hints.message: Plain text means the attacker sees exactly what users typed.
With hashing, attacker gets hashes (useless without cracking). With salt, even same passwords look different.
All answers are yes. Plain text = total compromise. Hash+salt = attacker must crack each individually.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Salt Purpose
practice_exercises.description: Explain why each salt scenario is secure or insecure.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. SECURE - unique salt means each hash is unique
2. INSECURE - shared salt means same passwords = same hashes
3. SECURE - salt is NOT secret, it just adds uniqueness
4. INSECURE - predictable, enables targeted rainbow tables
5. INSECURE - rainbow tables can crack common passwords instantly

hints.message: Salt must be: unique per user, random, stored with the hash. It does NOT need to be secret.
Shared salt = same passwords produce same hashes. No salt = rainbow tables work.
1.SECURE 2.INSECURE(shared) 3.SECURE(salt is public) 4.INSECURE(predictable) 5.INSECURE(rainbow tables)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: User Enumeration Risk
practice_exercises.description: Identify which error messages leak information.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. SAFE - generic, reveals nothing
2. UNSAFE - confirms alice does not exist
3. UNSAFE - confirms alice EXISTS but password is wrong
4. SAFE - generic
5. UNSAFE - confirms no account for that email
6. SAFE - generic

hints.message: An attacker can use different error messages to discover which usernames exist.
Same message for 'user not found' and 'wrong password' prevents enumeration.
SAFE: 1,4,6 (generic). UNSAFE: 2,3,5 (reveal whether account exists).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Session Security Checklist
practice_exercises.description: Check each session property as SECURE or INSECURE.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. INSECURE - predictable, contains username
2. SECURE - random, unpredictable
3. INSECURE - visible in URL, browser history, logs
4. SECURE - HttpOnly prevents JS access, Secure requires HTTPS
5. INSECURE - stolen sessions work forever
6. INSECURE - session fixation vulnerability
7. SECURE - prevents session reuse after logout

hints.message: Session IDs must be: random, in cookies (not URLs), with security attributes, expiring, destroyed on logout.
Predictable IDs can be guessed. URL sessions get logged. No expiry means stolen sessions last forever.
1.INSECURE 2.SECURE 3.INSECURE 4.SECURE 5.INSECURE 6.INSECURE 7.SECURE

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Least Privilege Scenarios
practice_exercises.description: Apply minimum permissions for each user type.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
VIEWER:  YES NO  NO  NO  NO  NO
EDITOR:  YES YES YES NO  NO  NO
ADMIN:   YES YES YES YES YES YES

hints.message: Least privilege: each role gets the MINIMUM it needs.
Viewers only read their own. Editors read all and write own. Admins get everything.
VIEWER: read own only. EDITOR: read all + write own. ADMIN: everything.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Trace the Login Flow
practice_exercises.description: Order the steps of a secure login process.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. User submits username and password
2. Server checks brute-force lockout status
3. Server looks up user record by username
4. Server hashes submitted password with stored salt
5. Server compares computed hash with stored hash
6. Server creates session with user ID and role
7. Server returns session ID as HttpOnly cookie
8. User is redirected to dashboard

hints.message: Start with what the user does, then follow server-side steps.
Check lockout first (before any work), then lookup, hash, compare, create session, return cookie.
Order: submit -> lockout check -> lookup -> hash -> compare -> create session -> set cookie -> redirect

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: IDOR Identification
practice_exercises.description: Find the IDOR vulnerability in each code snippet.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build your foundation in authentication and access control!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. YES - no ownership check, any user can view any profile by changing the ID
2. NO - uses session to determine user, not client-supplied ID
3. NO - ownership check present (ownerId == userId)
4. YES - any logged-in user can delete any other user (missing authz + IDOR)

hints.message: IDOR occurs when client-supplied IDs are used without verifying ownership.
Q1: id from URL, no check. Q2: id from session (safe). Q3: has check. Q4: id from URL, no role check.
1.YES(no check) 2.NO(from session) 3.NO(has check) 4.YES(no authz)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Generate a Salt
practice_exercises.description: Write a method that generates a cryptographically secure salt.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Salt 1: (24-char Base64 string)
Salt 2: (different 24-char string)
Different: true
Length: 16 bytes

hints.message: new SecureRandom().nextBytes(array) fills with random bytes.
byte[] salt = new byte[16]; new SecureRandom().nextBytes(salt); return Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(salt);
static String generateSalt(){byte[] s=new byte[16];new SecureRandom().nextBytes(s);return Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(s);}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Hash a Password
practice_exercises.description: Implement SHA-256 hashing with salt.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
(consistent hash for MyPassword)
(same hash - deterministic)
(different hash)

hints.message: MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-256"), then md.update(salt), then md.digest(password.getBytes()).
Decode salt from Base64, create MessageDigest, update with salt bytes, digest password bytes, encode result as Base64.
byte[] s=Base64.getDecoder().decode(saltB64); MessageDigest md=MessageDigest.getInstance("SHA-256"); md.update(s); return Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(md.digest(password.getBytes("UTF-8")));

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Verify a Password
practice_exercises.description: Check a submitted password against stored hash and salt.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Correct: true
Wrong: false

hints.message: Hash the input with the stored salt, then compare the result with the stored hash.
Same process as registration: decode salt, hash input+salt, compare with stored.
Hash input+salt, compare with storedHashB64 using .equals().

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Password Strength Checker
practice_exercises.description: Validate password meets minimum security requirements.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
false
false
false
false
false

hints.message: Check each requirement with regex or character iteration.
password.length()>=8 && password.matches(".*[A-Z].*") && matches lower && matches digit && matches special.
return p!=null && p.length()>=8 && p.matches(".*[A-Z].*") && p.matches(".*[a-z].*") && p.matches(".*[0-9].*") && p.matches(".*[!@#$%^&*].*");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Find Hard-coded Credentials
practice_exercises.description: Scan code for CWE-798 violations.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SAFE: String password = System.getenv("DB_PASS")...
CWE-798: if (input.equals("admin123")) return true...
CWE-798: String apiKey = "sk-live-abc123def456"...
SAFE: String secret = config.getProperty("app.secret")...
CWE-798: conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, "root"...

hints.message: Look for literal strings assigned to password/secret variables or used in .equals() comparisons.
Lines 2, 3, 5 have literal credentials. Lines 1, 4 use env vars / config files.
SAFE, CWE-798, CWE-798, SAFE, CWE-798

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Fix Hard-coded Password
practice_exercises.description: Refactor code to use environment variables.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vulnerable: true
Fixed approach: read from System.getenv("ADMIN_PASS")

hints.message: Replace the literal string with System.getenv("ADMIN_PASS").
String stored = System.getenv("ADMIN_PASS"); return stored != null && stored.equals(password);
static boolean fixedVerifyAdmin(String p){String s=System.getenv("ADMIN_PASS");return s!=null&&s.equals(p);}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Common Password Blacklist
practice_exercises.description: Reject passwords that appear in common password lists.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
REJECTED: Common password
REJECTED: Common password
ACCEPTED
REJECTED: Common password

hints.message: Convert to lowercase before checking the set.
if(COMMON.contains(password.toLowerCase())) return "REJECTED: Common password";
return COMMON.contains(password.toLowerCase()) ? "REJECTED: Common password" : "ACCEPTED";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Registration Flow
practice_exercises.description: Build a complete user registration with validation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SUCCESS: alice registered
ERROR: Username already taken
ERROR: Password too weak
ERROR: Username required

hints.message: Check username first (not empty, not in map), then password strength, then generate salt and hash.
if empty username -> error. if users.containsKey -> error. if weak password -> error. else generate salt, hash, store.
Check all validations before storing. Generate salt with SecureRandom, hash with SHA-256, store in map.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Timing-Safe Password Comparison
practice_exercises.description: Use constant-time comparison for password verification.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Safe (match): true
Safe (differ): false

hints.message: MessageDigest.isEqual(a.getBytes(), b.getBytes()) is constant-time.
Check for nulls first, then use MessageDigest.isEqual.
if(a==null||b==null) return false; return MessageDigest.isEqual(a.getBytes(), b.getBytes());

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Password Reset Token
practice_exercises.description: Generate and validate a secure password reset token.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Password security is the first line of defence!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Generated: kX7vR2mP9q...
Valid: true
Fake: false

hints.message: generateResetToken: SecureRandom 32 bytes, Base64 encode, store in map with expiry.
validateToken: check map contains token AND System.currentTimeMillis() < expiry.
Generate: byte[32], SecureRandom, Base64 URL encode, store with currentTimeMillis+3600000. Validate: get expiry, check not null and not expired.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Create a Secure Session ID
practice_exercises.description: Generate a cryptographically random session identifier.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Session 1: (43-char random)
Session 2: (different 43-char)
Unique: true
Length: 43

hints.message: Same pattern as CSRF tokens: SecureRandom + Base64 URL-safe.
byte[32], SecureRandom.nextBytes, Base64.getUrlEncoder().withoutPadding().encodeToString.
byte[] b=new byte[32]; new SecureRandom().nextBytes(b); return Base64.getUrlEncoder().withoutPadding().encodeToString(b);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Session Timeout Check
practice_exercises.description: Implement absolute and idle timeout validation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
VALID
EXPIRED: Idle timeout (30 min inactivity)
EXPIRED: Absolute timeout (8 hour max)

hints.message: Check absolute first (now - createdAt > ABS), then idle (now - lastAccess > IDLE).
if(now-createdAt > ABS_TIMEOUT) -> absolute expired. if(now-lastAccess > IDLE_TIMEOUT) -> idle expired. else valid.
if(now-createdAt>ABS_TIMEOUT) return "EXPIRED: Absolute timeout (8 hour max)"; if(now-lastAccess>IDLE_TIMEOUT) return "EXPIRED: Idle timeout (30 min inactivity)"; return "VALID";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Session Store
practice_exercises.description: Build a simple in-memory session store with CRUD operations.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!

hints.message: Use HashMap for session data. create puts, get retrieves, destroy removes.
create: sessions.put(id, Map.of("userId",userId,"role",role)). get: sessions.get(id). destroy: sessions.remove(id).
create: sessions.put(id, new HashMap<>(Map.of("userId",userId,"role",role))). get: return sessions.get(id). destroy: sessions.remove(id).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Session Fixation Prevention
practice_exercises.description: Generate new session ID after login.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vulnerable: attacker-known-id
Secure: (new random session ID)

hints.message: Remove the old session, generate a new random ID, create the session with the new ID.
sessions.remove(oldSessionId); generate new random ID; sessions.put(newId, user); return newId;
Remove old, generate 32-byte random, Base64 encode, store new session, return new ID.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Secure Cookie Builder
practice_exercises.description: Build a Set-Cookie header with all security attributes.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output: Set-Cookie: session=abc123; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/; Max-Age=28800

hints.message: Concatenate all attributes with semicolons.
return "Set-Cookie: session="+id+"; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/; Max-Age=28800";
Exact: "Set-Cookie: session="+sessionId+"; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Path=/; Max-Age=28800"

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Logout Handler
practice_exercises.description: Properly destroy session and clear cookie on logout.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before: 1 sessions
Session destroyed. Set-Cookie: session=; Max-Age=0; HttpOnly; Secure; Path=/
After: 0 sessions

hints.message: Remove from map + return a cookie with Max-Age=0 to clear it.
sessions.remove(sessionId); return clearing cookie header.
sessions.remove(sessionId); return "Session destroyed. Set-Cookie: session=; Max-Age=0; HttpOnly; Secure; Path=/";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Concurrent Session Limit
practice_exercises.description: Allow only one active session per user.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
New session: (id1)
New session: (id2), old session invalidated
Old session valid: false

hints.message: Check if user already has a session. If yes, remove it. Then create new.
String existing = userToSession.get(user); if(existing!=null) sessionToUser.remove(existing); then create new.
Remove old session if exists, generate new ID, update both maps.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Session Data Encryption
practice_exercises.description: Encrypt sensitive session data before storage.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Encrypted: (Base64 ciphertext)...
Decrypted: userId=alice,role=ADMIN

hints.message: Use Cipher.getInstance("AES") with ENCRYPT_MODE and DECRYPT_MODE.
encrypt: Cipher.init(ENCRYPT_MODE, key), doFinal(data.getBytes()), Base64 encode. decrypt: reverse.
Cipher c=Cipher.getInstance("AES"); c.init(Cipher.ENCRYPT_MODE,key); return Base64.getEncoder().encodeToString(c.doFinal(data.getBytes()));

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Session Activity Logger
practice_exercises.description: Log all session events for security monitoring.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
[timestamp] session=kX7vR2mP... event=CREATED detail=user=alice
[timestamp] session=kX7vR2mP... event=ACCESS detail=page=/profile
[timestamp] session=kX7vR2mP... event=DESTROYED detail=logout

hints.message: Use sessionId.substring(0, 8) + "..." to avoid logging full session IDs.
String partial = sessionId.substring(0, 8) + "..."; Build log entry with timestamp.
log.add("[" + java.time.LocalDateTime.now() + "] session=" + sessionId.substring(0,8) + "... event=" + event + " detail=" + detail);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Validate Session Middleware
practice_exercises.description: Build middleware that checks session on every request.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sessions keep users logged in securely. Let's build robust session handling!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ALLOW: /login is public
401: No session cookie
ALLOW: alice (ADMIN) accessing /profile
401: Invalid session

hints.message: Check if path is public first. Then check if cookie exists. Then validate against session store.
Public paths: /login, /register -> allow. No cookie -> 401. Invalid session -> 401. Valid -> allow with user info.
if(path is public) allow. if(cookie==null) 401. Map data=sessions.get(cookie); if(data==null) 401. else allow with user info.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Build RBAC Permission Check
practice_exercises.description: Implement a role-permission lookup system.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
true
false
true
false

hints.message: Get the permission set for the role, then check if it contains the action.
Set<String> p = perms.get(role); return p != null && p.contains(action);
return perms.containsKey(role) && perms.get(role).contains(action);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: IDOR Prevention
practice_exercises.description: Add ownership checks to a data access method.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ACCESS GRANTED: doc1 (owner)
ACCESS DENIED: doc2 belongs to bob
ACCESS GRANTED: doc2 (admin)
NOT FOUND: doc99

hints.message: Check if doc exists, then if user is owner OR role is ADMIN.
String owner=docOwners.get(docId); if null->not found. if owner.equals(userId) or ADMIN -> grant. else deny.

String o=docOwners.get(docId); if(o==null) return "NOT FOUND"; if(o.equals(userId)) return "ACCESS GRANTED (owner)"; if("ADMIN".equals(role)) return "ACCESS GRANTED (admin)"; return "ACCESS DENIED: belongs to "+o;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Authorisation Middleware
practice_exercises.description: Check permissions before processing any request.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ALLOW: ADMIN can DELETE /users/5
DENY: USER cannot DELETE /users/5
ALLOW: USER can GET /profile
DENY: No role (not authenticated)

hints.message: Check role is not null, get permission set, check if method is in the set.
if(role==null) deny. Set perms=rolePerms.get(role); if(perms==null or !contains method) deny. else allow.
if(role==null) return "DENY: No role"; Set<String> p=rolePerms.get(role); if(p==null||!p.contains(method)) return "DENY"; return "ALLOW";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Privilege Escalation Prevention
practice_exercises.description: Prevent users from promoting themselves.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
DENIED: Only admins can change roles
DENIED: Cannot assign role equal to or above your own
SUCCESS: bob changed to MANAGER
DENIED: Cannot change own role

hints.message: Check: is requester ADMIN? Is target != requester? Is newRole level < requester's level?
Three checks in order: role==ADMIN, target!=requester, levels.get(newRole)<levels.get(requesterRole).
if(!"ADMIN".equals(userRoles.get(requester))) deny. if(requester.equals(target)) deny. if(levels.get(newRole)>=levels.get(userRoles.get(requester))) deny. else change and return success.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Horizontal Access Control
practice_exercises.description: Prevent users from accessing other users' data.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
alice's orders: [ORD-1, ORD-2]
DENIED: alice cannot view bob's orders

hints.message: Check if requestUser equals targetUser.
if(!requestUser.equals(targetUser)) deny. else return orders.
if(!requestUser.equals(targetUser)) return "DENIED: "+requestUser+" cannot view "+targetUser+"'s orders"; return targetUser+"'s orders: "+userOrders.get(targetUser);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Vertical Access Control
practice_exercises.description: Enforce hierarchical permissions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ALLOW: USER (level 1) >= 1 for view_profile
DENY: USER (level 1) < 3 for manage_users
ALLOW: ADMIN (level 3) >= 3 for manage_users
ALLOW: MANAGER (level 2) >= 2 for view_reports

hints.message: Get the user's level from the map, compare with required level.
int userLevel = levels.getOrDefault(userRole, 0); if(userLevel >= requiredLevel) allow else deny.
int ul=levels.getOrDefault(userRole,0); return ul>=requiredLevel ? "ALLOW" : "DENY";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Admin Function Protection
practice_exercises.description: Add authentication check to critical admin functions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vulnerable: All passwords reset!
Secure (admin): All passwords reset!
Secure (user): DENIED: Admin access required
Secure (none): DENIED: Not authenticated

hints.message: Check session exists (authn), then check role is ADMIN (authz).
if(sessionId==null) not authenticated. if(!"ADMIN".equals(sessions.get(sessionId))) not authorised.
if(sessionId==null) return "DENIED: Not authenticated"; String role=sessions.get(sessionId); if(!"ADMIN".equals(role)) return "DENIED: Admin access required"; return "All passwords reset!";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Resource-Level Permissions
practice_exercises.description: Implement per-resource access control.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ALLOW: alice has WRITE on project-alpha
DENY: bob lacks WRITE on project-alpha
ALLOW: bob has READ on project-alpha
DENY: charlie has no access to project-alpha

hints.message: Navigate the nested map: acl -> resource -> user -> permissions set.
Map<String,Set<String>> resAcl = acl.get(resource); if null, deny. Set<String> userPerms = resAcl.get(user); if null or !contains, deny.
Get resource ACL, get user perms within it, check contains. Null at any level = deny.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Complete Access Check Pipeline
practice_exercises.description: Combine authentication + authorisation + IDOR check.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
401: Not authenticated
403: USER cannot DELETE
403: bob cannot access doc1 (owner: alice)
200: bob reads doc2
200: alice (ADMIN) deletes doc2

hints.message: Three checks in order: session exists, role allows action, ownership (or admin bypass).
Step 1: if no session -> 401. Step 2: if action needs admin and role!=ADMIN -> 403. Step 3: if not admin and not owner -> 403. Else 200.
Check session null->401. Get role/user. If adminActions.contains(action) and role!=ADMIN -> 403. If role!=ADMIN and owner!=user -> 403. Else 200.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Audit Trail for Access Decisions
practice_exercises.description: Log every access control decision.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Access control is the #1 web vulnerability. Let's get it right!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== AUDIT LOG ===
[ALLOW] user=alice role=ADMIN action=DELETE resource=/users/5
[DENY] user=bob role=USER action=DELETE resource=/users/5
[ALLOW] user=bob role=USER action=READ resource=/profile

hints.message: Determine allowed based on role and action, then log the decision.
ADMIN can do everything. USER can only READ. Log format: [ALLOW/DENY] user=... role=... action=... resource=...
boolean allowed = "ADMIN".equals(role) || "READ".equals(action); log.add((allowed?"[ALLOW]":"[DENY]")+" user="+user+" role="+role+" action="+action+" resource="+resource); return allowed;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Fix: Login Accepts Empty Password
practice_exercises.description: Fix authentication that doesn't check the password.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vuln: true
Fixed: false
Fixed: true

hints.message: Must check BOTH username AND password.
if(user==null || pass==null || pass.isEmpty()) return false; then check credentials.
return "admin".equals(user) && "correctPass".equals(pass);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Fix: Missing Role Check on Delete
practice_exercises.description: Add authorisation to a vulnerable endpoint.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
DENIED: Admin role required
Deleted: item1

hints.message: Check role before performing the action.
if(!"ADMIN".equals(role)) return deny. else remove and return success.
if(!"ADMIN".equals(role)) return "DENIED: Admin role required"; items.remove(item); return "Deleted: "+item;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Fix: Predictable Session IDs
practice_exercises.description: Replace sequential IDs with secure random ones.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Bad: session_1
Bad: session_2
Secure: (43-char random string)

hints.message: Use SecureRandom + 32 bytes + Base64 instead of a counter.
byte[] b=new byte[32]; new SecureRandom().nextBytes(b); Base64 encode.
byte[] b=new byte[32]; new java.security.SecureRandom().nextBytes(b); return java.util.Base64.getUrlEncoder().withoutPadding().encodeToString(b);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Fix: IDOR in Profile View
practice_exercises.description: Add ownership check to profile endpoint.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vuln: Bob's data
Fixed: Alice's data
Fixed: DENIED: Not your profile

hints.message: Check if currentUser's ID matches the requested profileId.
String ownId = userIds.get(currentUser); if(!ownId.equals(profileId)) deny.
String ownId=userIds.get(currentUser); if(!ownId.equals(profileId)) return "DENIED: Not your profile"; return profiles.getOrDefault(profileId,"Not found");

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Fix: No Session Expiry
practice_exercises.description: Add timeout to sessions that never expire.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vuln: true
Fixed: false

hints.message: Get creation time, compare with current time, reject if older than MAX_AGE.
Long created=sessions.get(id); return created!=null && (System.currentTimeMillis()-created) <= MAX_AGE;
Long c=sessions.get(id); return c!=null && (System.currentTimeMillis()-c)<=MAX_AGE;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Fix: Plain Text Password Storage
practice_exercises.description: Refactor to use hashing with salt.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Plain text stored: secret
Hash stored: (Base64 hash)...
Login: true
Wrong: false

hints.message: Register: generate 16-byte salt, hash password+salt, store both. Login: retrieve salt, hash input, compare.
SecureRandom for salt, MessageDigest SHA-256 for hash, Base64 for encoding.
Register: generate salt, hash with SHA-256, store [saltB64, hashB64]. Login: get stored salt/hash, hash input with stored salt, compare.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Fix: User Can Change Own Role
practice_exercises.description: Prevent self-service role escalation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vuln: alice is now ADMIN
DENIED: Only admins can change roles
SUCCESS: alice changed to MANAGER

hints.message: Check requester is ADMIN and requester != target.
if(!"ADMIN".equals(userRoles.get(requester))) deny. if(requester.equals(target)) deny self-change.

if(!"ADMIN".equals(userRoles.get(requester))) return "DENIED: Only admins can change roles"; if(requester.equals(target)) return "DENIED: Cannot change own role"; userRoles.put(target,newRole); return "SUCCESS: "+target+" changed to "+newRole;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Fix: No Lockout on Login
practice_exercises.description: Add brute-force protection to a vulnerable login.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
FAILED: 2 attempts remaining
FAILED: 1 attempts remaining
LOCKED: Account locked
LOCKED: Account locked

hints.message: Track fail count per user. After MAX, always return locked.
int f = fails.getOrDefault(u,0); if(f>=MAX) return locked. if wrong password, increment and return failed. if correct and not locked, return success.

int f=fails.getOrDefault(u,0); if(f>=MAX) return "LOCKED"; if(!users.getOrDefault(u,"").equals(p)){fails.put(u,f+1);if(f+1>=MAX) return "LOCKED"; return "FAILED: "+(MAX-f-1)+" remaining";} return "SUCCESS";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Fix: Admin Page Without Auth
practice_exercises.description: Add authentication to an unprotected admin endpoint.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Vuln: Admin Dashboard: 500 users, 12 pending requests
401: Authentication required
403: Admin access required
Admin Dashboard: 500 users, 12 pending requests

hints.message: Check session first (authn), then role (authz).
if(sessionId==null) 401. if(!ADMIN) 403. else return dashboard.
if(sessionId==null) return "401: Authentication required"; if(!"ADMIN".equals(role)) return "403: Admin access required"; return "Admin Dashboard: 500 users, 12 pending requests";

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Fix: Logging Passwords
practice_exercises.description: Remove sensitive data from audit logs.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the security bug and fix it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
BAD:
LOGIN user=alice pass=MySecret123 result=true
FIXED:
LOGIN user=alice result=SUCCESS
LOGIN user=alice result=FAILED

hints.message: Simply remove the password parameter from the log output.
System.out.println("LOGIN user=" + user + " result=" + (success ? "SUCCESS" : "FAILED"));
Never include passwords, tokens, or session IDs in logs. Log user + result only.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Build: Complete Login System
practice_exercises.description: Full registration + login + session + logout.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Register: SUCCESS
Login: Session=kX7vR2...
Check: {user=alice, role=ADMIN}
Logout: Session destroyed

hints.message: Four methods: register (hash+store), login (verify+create session), checkSession (lookup), logout (destroy).
register: salt+hash+store. login: lookup user, hash input, compare, create session. checkSession: sessions.get. logout: sessions.remove.
Full implementation: register with salt+SHA256, login with verification+SecureRandom session, checkSession returns map, logout removes.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Build: API Key Authentication
practice_exercises.description: Implement API key generation and validation.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Generated: sk_live_kX7vR2mP...
Validate: alice
Revoke: Key revoked
After revoke: null

hints.message: generateKey: SecureRandom prefix + random, store mapping. validateKey: lookup. revokeKey: remove.
Prefix with 'sk_live_' for readability. Generate 32 random bytes. Store key->userId mapping.
Full: generate with prefix, store in map, validate by lookup, revoke by removal.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Build: Rate Limiter
practice_exercises.description: Create a request rate limiter by IP address.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Request 1: true
Request 2: true
Request 3: true
Request 4: true
Request 5: true
Request 6: false
Request 7: false

hints.message: Track timestamps per IP. Remove old ones outside window. Count remaining.
Get list for IP, remove entries older than WINDOW_MS, if size < MAX add current time and return true, else return false.
Get/create list, remove old entries, check size < MAX, add current timestamp if allowed.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Build: Password Reset Flow
practice_exercises.description: Complete reset: request, validate token, update password.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Reset requested for alice@mail.com
Token: kX7vR2...
Reset complete: password updated
Replay: INVALID (token consumed)

hints.message: requestReset: generate token, store with email+expiry. completeReset: validate token, check expiry, update password, remove token.
Token should be single-use (removed after success) and time-limited.
Full flow: generate 32-byte token, store hashed version, send plain to email, validate by hashing input and comparing, remove after use.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Build: JWT-like Token Validator
practice_exercises.description: Create and validate signed authentication tokens.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Token: (payload.signature)
Valid: userId=alice,role=ADMIN
Tampered: INVALID

hints.message: Create: HMAC-SHA256(payload, secret) -> concatenate payload.signature. Validate: split, recompute HMAC, compare.
Mac mac = Mac.getInstance("HmacSHA256"); mac.init(new SecretKeySpec(SECRET.getBytes(), "HmacSHA256")); return Base64 of mac.doFinal.
createToken: compute HMAC of payload, return payload+"."+base64(hmac). validateToken: split on ".", recompute HMAC of part[0], compare with part[1].

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Build: Two-Factor Login
practice_exercises.description: Complete login requiring password + MFA code.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Step 1: Password verified for alice
Step 2: MFA code sent: 847293
Step 3: MFA verified: true
Step 4: Session created: kX7v...

Failed MFA:
Step 1: Password verified
Step 3: Wrong code: false
No session created

hints.message: Four separate steps. Session only created after both succeed.
Password check with hash. MFA with SecureRandom 6-digit code. Session with SecureRandom ID.
Full flow: verify hash, generate 6-digit code, verify code, create session only if both pass.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Build: Access Control Matrix
practice_exercises.description: Create a complete permission matrix checker.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== ACCESS MATRIX ===
          READ  WRITE DELETE ADMIN
ADMIN     YES   YES   YES    YES
MANAGER   YES   YES   NO     NO
USER      YES   NO    NO     NO

alice(ADMIN)  READ=ALLOW  DELETE=ALLOW
bob(USER)     READ=ALLOW  DELETE=DENY

hints.message: Three maps: roles->permissions, users->roles. checkAccess: look up role, check permission set.
Use Map<String,Set<String>> for role permissions, Map<String,String> for user roles.
Full: define 3+ roles with permission sets, assign users, implement checkAccess with role lookup, printMatrix showing grid.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Build: Security Event Monitor
practice_exercises.description: Detect suspicious authentication patterns.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== SECURITY ALERTS ===
[BRUTE FORCE] User 'alice' has 10 failed logins
[CREDENTIAL STUFFING] IP 45.33.22.1 failed 20 logins across 15 accounts
[ACCOUNT TAKEOVER] User 'bob' password changed, then login from new IP

hints.message: Aggregate events by user and IP. Flag when counts exceed thresholds.
Group by user for brute force, by IP for credential stuffing, track password changes + new IPs for takeover.
recordEvent adds to list. detectBruteForce counts fails per user. detectCredentialStuffing counts fails per IP. detectAccountTakeover tracks password change then login from new IP.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Build: Complete Secure API
practice_exercises.description: Full API with auth, authz, IDOR, rate limiting, and logging.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== SECURE API DEMO ===
Register alice (ADMIN): SUCCESS
Login alice: Session=kX7v...
Admin views users: ALLOW
User views admin page: DENY
User views other's data: DENY (IDOR blocked)
Rate limit exceeded: BLOCKED
Audit log: 6 entries recorded

hints.message: Combine all previous skills: hashing, sessions, RBAC, IDOR checks, rate limiting, logging.
Build each component separately, then wire them together in the main method.
Full system: user store with hashing, session store, RBAC permissions, ownership checks, request counter per IP, event logging.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Build: Penetration Test Checklist
practice_exercises.description: Automated security assessment for authentication systems.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build complete security systems from the ground up!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== AUTH SECURITY ASSESSMENT ===
[PASS] Passwords hashed with salt
[PASS] No hard-coded credentials
[PASS] Generic login error messages
[PASS] Brute-force lockout
[PASS] Secure session IDs
[PASS] Session expiry
[PASS] Session destroyed on logout
[PASS] RBAC enforced
[PASS] IDOR prevented
[PASS] Privilege escalation blocked
[PASS] Admin functions protected
[PASS] Audit logging active
Score: 12/12

hints.message: Write each test as a boolean check on the system under test.
Each test calls a function and checks the result. Use the test() helper method.
12 tests: hash check, no hardcoded, generic errors, lockout, random session, expiry, logout, RBAC, IDOR, escalation, admin protection, audit.
